channel.title: The Comp Desk — Learn
channel.description: Plain-language guides to New York workers' compensation — benefits, process, injuries, and your rights. By The Comp Desk Editorial Team.

item.title: How to Choose a NY Workers' Comp Attorney
item.description: The right workers' comp attorney can mean tens of thousands of dollars more in benefits. Learn what credentials matter, what questions to ask, and what red flags to avoid when choosing a New York workers' comp lawyer.
item.dc:creator: The Comp Desk Editorial Team

item.title: How Long Do I Have to File a Workers' Comp Claim in New York?
item.description: New York has a two-year statute of limitations for workers' comp claims, with different rules for occupational disease and gradually-developing injuries. Learn the deadlines before it's too late.
item.dc:creator: The Comp Desk Editorial Team

item.title: Can My Employer Fire Me for Filing a Workers' Comp Claim?
item.description: New York WCL §120 prohibits retaliation for filing a workers' comp claim. Learn what counts as retaliation, how to prove it, and what to do if your employer retaliates against you.
item.dc:creator: The Comp Desk Editorial Team

item.title: Knee Injuries and Workers' Comp
item.description: Knee injuries — torn meniscus, ACL tears, knee replacements — result in Schedule Loss of Use awards in New York workers' comp. Learn how the knee is evaluated and what affects your SLU percentage.
item.dc:creator: The Comp Desk Editorial Team

item.title: Back Injuries and Workers' Comp
item.description: Back injuries are the #1 workers' comp claim in New York. Learn how herniated discs, radiculopathy, and spinal injuries are handled in the workers' comp system — benefits, permanency, and what to watch for.
item.dc:creator: The Comp Desk Editorial Team

item.title: What Happens at a Workers' Comp Hearing?
item.description: Workers' comp hearings happen before a Workers' Compensation Law Judge at a Board district office. Learn who is in the room, the types of hearings, how they proceed, and what outcomes are possible.
item.dc:creator: The Comp Desk Editorial Team

item.title: Section 32 Settlements: What to Know Before You Sign
item.description: A Section 32 Waiver Agreement permanently closes your workers' comp case. Before you accept any offer, understand what you are giving up, how the math works, and when settlement makes sense.
item.dc:creator: The Comp Desk Editorial Team

item.title: Surviving an Independent Medical Exam
item.description: The IME doctor is hired and paid by the insurance carrier. Learn how to prepare for your Independent Medical Exam, what to say, and how to fight back against an unfavorable report.
item.dc:creator: The Comp Desk Editorial Team

item.title: How to File a C-3 Form in New York
item.description: The C-3 Employee Claim form starts your workers' comp case at the Board. Learn how to file it correctly, avoid common mistakes, and meet the two-year deadline.
item.dc:creator: The Comp Desk Editorial Team

item.title: Schedule Loss of Use Awards Explained
item.description: A Schedule Loss of Use award compensates for permanent partial loss of function of a scheduled body part. Learn how SLU percentages are assigned and how to calculate your award in New York workers' comp.
item.dc:creator: The Comp Desk Editorial Team

item.title: Temporary Total vs. Temporary Partial Disability: What's the Difference?
item.description: TT and TPD are the two types of weekly disability benefits in New York workers' comp. Learn the difference, how each is calculated, and what changes when you return to light duty.
item.dc:creator: The Comp Desk Editorial Team

item.title: What Is Your Average Weekly Wage and Why Does It Matter?
item.description: Your Average Weekly Wage is the foundation of every New York workers' comp benefit calculation. Learn how it's computed, where mistakes happen, and how to verify yours is correct.
item.dc:creator: The Comp Desk Editorial Team
